title: Same Pre-training Loss, Better Downstream: Implicit Bias Matters for Language Models

structured.summary: This paper investigates the implicit bias induced by optimization dynamics in language model pre-training, demonstrating that among models with the same minimal pre-training loss, differences in downstream transferability arise due to implicit preference for flatter minima driven by SGD noise. Through controlled synthetic experiments, empirical Hessian trace estimation, and theoretical analysis on a synthetic Dyck language, the authors connect flatness to improved downstream generalization and feature learning, highlighting the role of model size and training algorithm in shaping these effects.

strengths.point: Insightful connection between SGD noise and implicit bias towards flat minima in language modeling
strengths.evidence: Theoretical analysis (Theorem 4.3) rigorously characterizes SGD dynamics as implicitly minimizing Hessian trace on the global minimizer manifold.

strengths.point: Empirical demonstration that flatness correlates strongly with downstream transferability beyond pre-training loss
strengths.evidence: Figures 1, 2, 3, 5 and Table 2 show consistent correlations between lower Hessian trace and improved downstream accuracy across multiple synthetic datasets and model sizes.

strengths.point: Provable characterization in a synthetic Dyck language setting illustrating that flat minima correspond to solutions with meaningful syntactic feature learning
strengths.evidence: Theorem 6.1 proves that the flattest solutions recover transferable syntactic representations enabling zero downstream loss, distinguishing them from memorization.

strengths.point: Systematic exploration of the effects of training duration, optimization algorithm, and model size on downstream performance at fixed pre-training loss
strengths.evidence: Experiments show that longer training after loss saturation, AdamW vs adversarial training, and larger models yield flatter minima and better transfer.

weaknesses.point: Exclusive reliance on synthetic datasets and absence of experiments on natural language corpora or pretrained transformer architectures
weaknesses.evidence: All empirical results derive from PCFG, HMM, and OPT-generated synthetic data and relatively small non-transformer models, limiting real-world validity.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient engagement with prior work on flat minima and transferability in self-supervised and large language models
weaknesses.evidence: Key references on flatness in contrastive self-supervised learning and transformer flatness analyses (e.g., Grill et al. 2020, Izmailov et al. 2018) are not discussed, hindering clarity on novelty.

weaknesses.point: Limited ablation and robustness analyses regarding choice of flatness metric, batch size, and optimizer variants
weaknesses.evidence: Only Hessian trace via stochastic estimation is used without comparison to alternative flatness measures or systematic variation of noise scale or optimizer types.

weaknesses.point: Lack of detailed hyperparameter, architecture, and Hessian estimation implementation details impedes reproducibility
weaknesses.evidence: Descriptions of model sizes are vague, and stochastic trace estimator parameters are omitted.

weaknesses.point: Theoretical results focus on vanilla SGD, but experiments use AdamW and adversarial training without bridging the gap
weaknesses.evidence: No discussion on how implicit bias theory extends to adaptive optimizers or adversarial methods, limiting practical relevance.

weaknesses.point: Downstream evaluation is restricted to synthetic latent label classification without standard NLP benchmarks or robustness analyses
weaknesses.evidence: No experiments on GLUE, SuperGLUE, or probing of real-world representations; also no study on fine-tuning stability or robustness benefits of flat minima.

questions.question: How do the implicit bias effects and flatness-transferability relationships observed in synthetic settings translate to large-scale pretrained transformer models on natural language data?
questions.motivation: Validating these findings on realistic LLMs is essential to assess practical impact and guide training strategies.

questions.question: To what extent do alternative flatness metrics or optimization hyperparameters (e.g., batch size, learning rate schedules) influence the implicit bias towards flatter minima and downstream generalization?
questions.motivation: Understanding robustness to metric choice and noise scale is critical for designing effective training algorithms.

questions.question: Can the theoretical framework be extended to encompass adaptive optimizers like AdamW and adversarial training dynamics used in practice?
questions.motivation: Bridging theory and practice would strengthen explanatory power and suggest improvements to current optimization methods.

critique_points.point_001: Exclusive reliance on synthetic datasets and absence of experiments on natural language corpora or pretrained transformer architectures. All empirical results derive from PCFG, HMM, and OPT-generated synthetic data and relatively small non-transformer models, limiting real-world validity
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient engagement with prior work on flat minima and transferability in self-supervised and large language models. Key references on flatness in contrastive self-supervised learning and transformer flatness analyses (e.g., Grill et al. 2020, Izmailov et al. 2018) are not discussed, hindering clarity on novelty
critique_points.point_003: Limited ablation and robustness analyses regarding choice of flatness metric, batch size, and optimizer variants. Only Hessian trace via stochastic estimation is used without comparison to alternative flatness measures or systematic variation of noise scale or optimizer types
critique_points.point_004: Lack of detailed hyperparameter, architecture, and Hessian estimation implementation details impedes reproducibility. Descriptions of model sizes are vague, and stochastic trace estimator parameters are omitted
critique_points.point_005: Theoretical results focus on vanilla SGD, but experiments use AdamW and adversarial training without bridging the gap. No discussion on how implicit bias theory extends to adaptive optimizers or adversarial methods, limiting practical relevance
critique_points.point_006: Downstream evaluation is restricted to synthetic latent label classification without standard NLP benchmarks or robustness analyses. No experiments on GLUE, SuperGLUE, or probing of real-world representations; also no study on fine-tuning stability or robustness benefits of flat minima
